title: Home & Family
shortSummary: Talk about your family members and describe your home in Turkish.
culturalNote: Family is the centre of Turkish life, and relationships extend warmly to neighbours and close friends, who may be addressed with family titles out of respect. Turkish has remarkably precise kinship words: your mother's brother is dayı while your father's brother is amca, and a maternal aunt (teyze) is distinct from a paternal aunt (hala). Older people are often addressed with abi (older brother) or abla (older sister) even when not related, as a sign of affection and respect. Elders are highly respected, and many households remain close-knit, with frequent visits and shared meals across generations. The word for home, ev, carries deep warmth; an invitation to someone's ev for tea is an act of genuine hospitality, and a guest is treated with great care. Possession is shown with suffixes, so annem means 'my mother' and evimiz means 'our home'.

keyPhrases.english: How many rooms do you have?

grammar.explanation: Instead of separate words, Turkish attaches possessive endings to the noun. The endings (with vowel harmony) are -(i)m (my), -(i)n (your, sen), -(s)i (his/her), -(i)miz (our), -(i)niz (your, siz), -leri (their). So ev (home) becomes evim (my home), evin (your home), evimiz (our home).

examples.en: Our home is in the city centre.

grammar.commonMistake: Using a separate word like benim alone. You still add the suffix: benim evim, not just benim ev.

grammar.rule: Expressing 'to have' with var and a possessive
grammar.explanation: Turkish says 'I have X' as 'my X exists'. You add a possessive ending to the thing and follow with var (or yok for 'don't have'): iki çocuğum var (I have two children, literally 'my two children exist'), bahçemiz var (we have a garden).
grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting the possessive ending on the owned noun. It is köpeğim var (I have a dog), not köpek var for 'I have a dog'.

dialogue.setup: Selin shows her new neighbour Okan a photo of her family.

lines.speaker: Selin
lines.english: Look Okan, this is my family.

lines.speaker: Selin
lines.native: Bu annem ve bu da babam.
lines.english: This is my mother and this is my father.

lines.speaker: Selin
lines.native: Evet, bir kardeşim var. Sen evli misin?
lines.english: Yes, I have one sibling. Are you married?

lines.speaker: Okan
lines.native: Hayır, bekarım. Ama büyük bir ailem var.
lines.english: No, I'm single. But I have a big family.

lines.speaker: Selin
lines.native: Çok güzel. Bir gün çay için buyurun!
lines.english: Very nice. Come over for tea one day!

quiz.question: How do you say 'my mother' in Turkish?
quiz.explanation: Anne (mother) plus the possessive -m gives annem.

quiz.question: Which means 'I have two children'?
quiz.explanation: Çocuğum (my child) plus var expresses 'I have children'.

quiz.question: Ev_____ büyük. (Our home is big)
quiz.explanation: The possessive -imiz on ev gives evimiz, 'our home'.

quiz.question: What does 'Bekarım' mean?
quiz.explanation: Bekar means single; the -ım ending means 'I am'.

quiz.question: Arrange: var / kardeşim / bir
quiz.explanation: Bir kardeşim var means 'I have one sibling'.
